question: Why is rendering all list items in a single observer component inefficient?

options.text: When one item changes, the entire list re-renders, not just the changed item
MobX can't track array elements inside a single observer
options.explanation: MobX tracks array elements regardless. The issue is that React re-renders the entire component, including all list items.

options.text: observer doesn't work with the map() method
options.explanation: observer works perfectly with map(). The problem isn't map() but that one observer component = one re-render block.

options.text: The list can only contain 100 elements
options.explanation: MobX doesn't limit the number of elements. The issue is re-render performance with a large number of elements.

question: What does intercept do when the handler returns null?

options.text: Cancels the change — the observable is not updated
Sets the value to null
options.explanation: Returning null from an intercept handler means 'cancel the change', not 'set to null'. The observable keeps its previous value.

options.text: Removes the property from the object
options.explanation: intercept doesn't remove properties. Returning null is a signal to cancel the change.

options.text: Throws a MobX error
options.explanation: Returning null is a legitimate way to cancel a change in intercept. There will be no error.

question: What is the key difference between intercept and observe?

options.text: intercept fires BEFORE the change is applied and can cancel it, observe fires AFTER and cannot
intercept works synchronously, observe works asynchronously
options.explanation: Both work synchronously. The difference is the timing: intercept before application, observe after.

options.text: intercept is for arrays, observe is for objects
options.explanation: Both work with arrays, objects, and individual properties. The difference is in timing and capabilities.

options.text: observe replaces autorun, and intercept replaces reaction
options.explanation: observe and intercept don't replace autorun/reaction. They're low-level APIs for intercepting (intercept) and observing (observe) changes to specific properties.

question: Why shouldn't you mutate other observables inside intercept?

options.text: Mutations inside intercept can lead to unpredictable update ordering and infinite loops
MobX will throw an error on any mutation inside intercept
options.explanation: MobX doesn't always throw an error, but mutations inside intercept are an anti-pattern because intercept is called before applying the current change, and mutations to other observables break predictability.

options.text: intercept doesn't have access to other observables
options.explanation: intercept is a regular JavaScript function with closure access. Technically you can access any observable, but mutating them inside intercept is dangerous.

options.text: This is a TypeScript limitation
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't restrict mutations inside intercept. This is an architectural rule of MobX: intercept is intended only for validating and modifying the current change object.

question: What tasks is observe best suited for?

options.text: Logging changes
Auditing — recording change history
Sending analytics when a specific property changes
Validating data before changes
options.explanation: For validation before changes, use intercept, not observe. observe fires AFTER the change — it can't be cancelled.

options.text: Blocking invalid values
options.explanation: observe can't block changes — it fires after they're applied. Use intercept for blocking.
